column1:
NEW LAUNCH-CRITICAL TICKET — GDPR-appropriate privacy policy, cookie consent banner, and data-subject request flow,"

Description

  • Context:
column2: Lawyer engagement is deferred. For launch, we follow a standard GDPR-appropriate privacy policy + cookie consent + data-subject-request setup — enough to be defensible, not reinventing the wheel. Every reputable supplement / DTC brand does this
column3:
we use an off-the-shelf template and adapt, rather than drafting from scratch.
  • Current behaviour:
column4:
No published privacy policy. No cookie consent banner. No data-subject-request (DSR) mechanism.
  • Expected behaviour:
column5:
• Privacy policy generated from a reputable multi-regime template (Termly, iubenda, or similar) covering UK GDPR + EU GDPR + POPIA as the baseline. Published at /privacy (or equivalent) and linked from every page footer, checkout, account creation, and email signatures.
  • Policy explicitly covers:
column6:
what we collect (IPs, behavioural data, order info, email, etc.), lawful basis for each, retention periods, third-party processors (Cloudflare, Stripe, email provider, etc.), cross-border transfers, user rights (access, deletion, portability, objection), cookie use.
  • Cookie consent banner — geolocation-aware. EU / UK / SA visitors get strict opt-in with granular toggles (necessary / analytics / marketing). Other regions get a lighter banner. Consent state persisted.
  • Data-subject request (DSR) mechanism — simple email address or form at /privacy-request (or in account settings) that routes to Monique / a designated privacy contact. DSRs must be actionable within 30 days (GDPR timeline).
  • Terms of Service + cookie policy published alongside the privacy policy.
  • How to test / acceptance criteria:
column7:
• Privacy policy live at /privacy, linked from footer + checkout + account flows + transactional emails
  • Cookie banner shows on first visit
column9:
granular toggles function
  • Analytics / tracking respects consent state (don't fire analytics if user declined)
  • DSR mechanism reachable and tested end-to-end (submit a test request, confirm it arrives at the inbox / form destination)
  • Policy coverage verified against UK GDPR + EU GDPR + POPIA checklists (use any of the free compliance-check tools)
  • Privacy policy references the IP posture defined in the sibling ticket
    • For Servaas:
column10:
Implementation of the consent banner + DSR mechanism + privacy-policy route.

column1:
NEW LAUNCH-CRITICAL TICKET — IP storage posture, hashing, and retention across the stack,"Description

Context:
column2:
Because we're shipping to UK / EU / SA customers, IP addresses count as personal data under UK GDPR, EU GDPR, and POPIA. We need a defined posture for how IPs are captured, stored, hashed, retained, and purged. This unblocks B5 (analyze popup), B9 (IP on user profile), B10 (audit log IP column), and the internal per-user profile ticket. Without a defined posture, we ship features that create legal exposure.
Current behaviour:
column3: Localization middleware uses IP for currency detection
column4:
unclear whether IP is stored anywhere queryable. No documented retention period. No hashing strategy. No purge routine. No admin policy on who can see raw IP.
Expected behaviour:
column5:
Raw IP capture allowed for short-term security and fraud-prevention window only — recommend 30–90 day rolling retention, configurable.
Hashed IP (one-way, salted SHA-256 or equivalent) stored for long-term analytics — this is what feeds B5, B9, B10, and the internal profile.
Derived region (country, optionally country + city) stored alongside the hashed IP for geographic analytics without exposing the raw address.
Admin surfaces (audit log, internal profile, analyze popup) show hashed IP + country by default. A separate, logged "
column7:
flow allows raw IP access for specific incident investigations — every reveal is itself logged with actor, reason, timestamp.
Automatic purge job runs daily, deleting raw IPs older than the retention window.
Anonymous-session stitching (from B9) keeps hashed IP linked to user_id once an account is created.
Lawful basis:
column8:
documented as legitimate interest (fraud prevention + security + product analytics), with balancing test recorded.
All of this must be disclosed in the privacy policy (sibling ticket below).


How to test / acceptance criteria:
column9: New visitor → raw IP captured with timestamp
column10:
hashed IP + country derived and stored
Retention window configurable
column11:
purge job verifiable (raw IP older than window is gone)
Admin surfaces show hashed IP + country by default
column12:
raw-IP reveal requires explicit action and is itself logged
Anonymous session stitches to user_id on account creation
column13:
hashed IP carries forward
Documented lawful basis + balancing test sits in a repo /privacy or similar location
Feature parity with B5, B9, B10, and internal profile tickets — all consume hashed IP + country, not raw



For Servaas:
column14:
This is the foundational privacy-engineering ticket. Every other IP-related feature depends on it. Please scope and size alongside B9 / B10 / internal profile so we don't build three features three different ways.

The new products.json (v1) only covers 5 products — the ones we have complete label info for. The remaining 9 lack supplier documentation for ingredients, dosages, allergens, and suitability. Rather than publish a JSON/dashboard pair that's ingredient-silent for those products, we're temporarily marking them "
